message:
> No, but that's only because counterfeiting causes inflation. Which negatively impacts me.

Counterfeiting causes devaluation of money (through inflation).  Pirating causes devaluation of the good (through much the same means - having more of something available decreases its price).  This devaluation of the good negatively impacts the creator.

Why is the former unacceptable to you, but the latter is fine?  If it's just because one negatively impacts you and the other doesn't, then you don't have a real moral argument, you are just being selfish.

message:
>>Morality - stealing is wrong. I don't think this requires much elaboration.

Stealing implies that something is lost. Piracy is a duplication of goods. Companies are not out goods they are selling because of people pirating only money.


>>The fines for illegal downloading can be up to $30,000 per song, or higher at the judge's discretion. You are effectively gambling a year of your life every time you download a song illegally.

The odds of this happening are a like a reverse lottery. Furthermore, a music company  would have to stand to make money by suing you, which they won't.

>>The only real barrier to prosecuting every single internet pirate is the Constitutional right to a jury trial. It is all recorded forever, it is all data, it can all be automated except for the jury trial that they have to give you even though your IP address effectively proves that it was you. You are banking on the government never making an exception to this principle, no matter how much artificial intelligence advances, and even though it would instantly make every major corporation millions and millions of dollars.

This isn't true, the other barrier to entry is the fact that they also have to prove a specific person did something autonomously. If a family member steals my computer and has access to my wifi and illegally downloads something, and courts come after me for it, they still have to prove *I* did it. If they can't they've already lost far more money then they can hope to reclaim on a worthless case. Never mind the cost of litigation itself.  What if I have no money to take?  The company will never recoup costs of 100% of the people they sue, even if they could 1 button click lawsuit everyone, which means the costs they *do recover* likely would be a loss anyway. But that's a technological pipedream anyway. I'd argue that this aspect of your argument, for the most part is extremely tinfoil hat.

@camel-director, I don't think it's that simple. Just because pirating doesn't directly take money from the creator's pocket, it doesn't mean it's not harming them. message:
I care a lot about free speech and I'm a moderator of this sub here. Censoring things doesn't mean you're against free speech at all. The people whose posts I remove from CMV are free to make identical posts elsewhere and I *strongly* believe they ought to have every right to do so.

There's nothing inconsistent with caring about free speech and then going and participating in or creating spaces where speech isn't absolutely free. Like, I wouldn't let some random dude into my house to hassle me and call me names. Does that mean I'm not in support of free speech? If so, it means literally no humans care about free speech, including those humans who codified the freedom into the Constitution of the United States.

There's this idea out there that every single space always needs to be open to literally all forms of speech all the time and frankly I can't fathom why anyone has this notion to begin with. Oh great, you've opened the floor to the loudest people who are themselves censoring everyone else by being loud and obnoxious yet we can't do anything about it lest we look like we don't care about free speech.

I don't have to tolerate racism in my workplace, I don't have to tolerate insults in my home, and I don't have to tolerate rule breakers in the subreddit I moderate. I can have these positions and still think that free speech is important and worth protecting.

@optical-offset, I see your point, but don't you think you're being a bit hypocritical by calling out Trump supporters for censorship when you're essentially doing the same thing by criticizing their spaces for not being open to all forms of speech? I mean, isn't that just a form of censorship in itself? @huge-compound makes a good point that just because a space isn't open to all forms of speech doesn't mean they're against free speech. What's your take on that?

message:
> It's a matter of perception: I've heard players call umpires idiots, morons, and railing at how stupid their actions are, and no response. 

I'm confused, isn't it a matter of measurements and claims.  We have a person claiming to be a victim which is a measurable position. You were making a claim that a whole group of people (men) get away with that same behavior.  I was just curious to hear how you came to that conclusion.  All you had to do was say it came from your personal experience.   It wasn't clear how you came to that determination.  Honestly, I was hoping to get some measurements or statistics from the league.

I believe the umpire over-reacted and acted out against a woman in a way he wouldn't (and hasn't this year) against a man.

First, Ramos is one of the few umpires who has chaired all four men's Grand Slam finals. He has well over 20 years of experience at the highest levels of tennis. He has dealt with coaching, outbursts, and verbal tantrums and until Saturday's women's final, had not assessed a game penalty since mid-2017. (ITA stats, but not complete). But I think we can agree Carlos Ramos is nowhere near a neophyte in the chair, and knows full well the pressures involved in Grand Slam tennis.

Second, Serena's behavior was not stellar, but it was not inconsistent with her past, nor with that of other players, male or female. If anything, her response on the ridiculous (my opinion) coaching call was direct and crossed no line. In the Federer-Thiem match, Thiem constantly looked at his coach, and even exchanged words with him several times. Federer did object to this obvious coaching to the umpire, who did nothing. Throughout this tournament, as with every tennis tournament, coaching happens. It is against the rules, but by and large, the rule is not enforced or it is not enforced fairly (so the rule should be abolished, but it still applies). Osaka's coach was doing the same as Serena's, but there was no warning there. In fact, Ramos's countryman, Rafa Nadal, did receive a coaching warning from Ramos and Nadal's response was to say he (Ramos) would never chair a match of his again. Serena said the same thing.

As for what players say to umpires, I have heard vile cursing, labels such as "idiot," "moron," and "stupid" and in this tournament, several male players screamed at the umpires for calling time faults (where there is no discretion or leeway: once time is up, it has to be called), as well as on the discretionary calls to over-rule and others.

Serena is also one of the few players who never uses the on-court coaching option, and due to problems with her father's open attempts to coach when they were younger, both she and Venus learned to not look at their box/coach. She was not looking when Ramos made the call. If the letter of law did not apply during the tournament, why apply it in the Final, and to only one player?

On the racket smash, male and female players smashed rackets all through the Open, and less than half (I don't have the stats, but based on my observation of about 35-40 matches) were issued warnings. Note I said "warnings." Even if the player had a previous fault called on them, indicating that the racket smash was then a point penalty, the umpires used their discretion to remind players rather than punish them. If the early rounds have the least-experienced umpires and they all successfully avoided escalating the situation (except for the guy who gave Kyrgios a pep talk), then should we (players and public) expect less of a seasoned top level umpire? Ramos could have easily issued a warning and let the incident pass.

On the verbal abuse, Serena, Evert and all the other women have it right: the men get away with much worse, in tone and content. Serena said "You took a point away from me. That makes you a thief, too." She is stating the correct view that the point penalty was a personal decision by the umpire, and in doing so, he took something from Serena that was not required to happen. And for this statement, Ramos then did something that I believe has never happened in a Grand Slam final in the Open era: a game penalty. 

I cannot say Serena is entirely blameless: She did not have to slam a racket, nor state her opinion of Ramos. But rules and common practice form an environment in which Serena's actions were not only tolerable (as shown throughout the tournament), they are practically the norm. Ramos was the one who upended the norm, at a Slam final, and destroyed the integrity and beauty of what should have been the eventual crowning (I think) of Osaka as the first Japanese player to win a Slam. I don't see Serena as the villain (in your less-charged form of the word), but as the victim of a personal animosity possibly based on gender, race, or some combination.

Pulling a gun / brandishing is merely a step on the use of force continuum.  It's a warning.  If you are ever in a similar situation (which I hope doesn't happen) you can take different steps.   Me personally, I'm not eager to rely on the generosity & mercy of those who are willing to threaten me over cash / goods.

So, question -- how does one determine "identity?" It's not something that can be proven nor disproven, just as I could tell you that my favourite colour is blue when it's actually purple, and there's really no way to know if I'm telling the truth.

Leaving aside gender roles (as not everyone conforms to them) and biological sex (as trans people exist), what does it *mean* to identify "as a man" or "as a woman?" As far as gender/sex dysphoria is concerned in the physical sense, I'm ready to agree with it from a layman's perspective, especially considering the existence of several other conditions affecting bodily intergrity and perception. My problem is in considering the social aspect of gender, as I can't see a way to define it without leaning on gender roles/stereotypes to detetmine which is one and which is the other. As far as "I want to be perceived as male/female" is concerned, it does make sense to me that one would prefer the pronouns and associations made of the target sex as a means of reaffirming to themselves that they are "passing" for a member of that sex; however this would be contigent on the presence of dysphoria, which I've heard is no longer considered a necessary aspect of "being transgender" (if this is actually a fringe position, please forgive me -- the only trans person I know IRL isn't involved in the community, so I have to base most of my information on what I've seen online).

The example of role models is interesting -- I personally have mostly female role models, as I can understand many of the struggles that they went through being women, as I am one myself. Given that men are dominant in my current line of work and (hopefully) future career, I'm not sure if I would have this same reasoning for male role models had I been a man instead. 

(FTR, since this is an emotionally-driven topic, I personally have no issues with respecting the wishes of trans people, this is simply one aspect of the theory that I have a hard time with.)

Gender roles: what we as a society expect people of a gender to look and act like. Women are nurturing, men are strong, boys love trucks, girls adore pink. Women wear make up, men don‘t. Men don‘t shave their legs, women do. A woman who is a truck driver, has short hair and plays football does not confirm to the stereotypical gender role of a woman.

Gender identity: the gender you identify as. It very often, but not always, matches the sex we‘re assigned at birth. It can also be discribed as the „sex of the brain“. It‘s essentially who you see yourself as, who you identify as and with (do you find yourself driven to female or male role models? Do you feel comfortable being „one of the boys“ or „one of the girls“? Who do you look at and go „I wanna look like them!“?). The truck driver woman in the first example identifies as a woman, even though she doesn‘t neccessarily confirm to the gender roles tied to that label.
